EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Smith River at Eden (EDSN7) crested at 12.55 feet late on the 18th in response to the heavy rains upstream. Minor flood stage is 11 feet. Flooding was confined to lowland areas. This was the highest reading at this gauge since January 25, 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wait for it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported numerous small to large slope failures developed along the Highway 176 corridor in Polk County after as much as 8 inches of rain fell across the western part of the county in just a few hours. Around 30 homes were damaged, with a couple destroyed. One 59-year-old woman died when her garage collapsed as she was trying to flee her home. Her husband was swept into Highway 176 but survived with mostly minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southeast flow developed northeast of subtropical cyclone Alberto, which made landfall across the western Florida Peninsula on the 28th and moved north across Alabama throughout the 29th. Resultant upslope flow into the southern Appalachians resulted in development of widespread heavy rain showers across western North Carolina throughout the 29th, with activity being most concentrated along the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge. By the time the showers tapered off during the 30th, total rainfall of 7-10 inches occurred along the Blue Ridge. This resulted in fairly widespread flash flooding followed by an extended period of high stream flow, with McDowell County being the hardest hit area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media, Em, Stream gauges, and other sources indicated significant flash flooding developed across mainly western McDowell county after 4 to 6 inches of rain fell during the morning and afternoon of the 29th. The Catawba River exceeded its established Flood Stage according to a gauge in pleasant gardens, and most of the issues were due to the river and its tributaries. The Catawba overflowed its banks near its headwaters in Old Fort, with much of the downtown roads flooded and water entering many businesses. Swift water rescues were required at a couple of locations in the Old Fort area, including on Bat Cave Rd and Catawba River Rd. Closer to Marion, a structure collapsed under the weight of flood water on Roby Conley Rd. Several tractor trailer trucks became stalled in flood water on Columbia Carolina Rd near Old Fort, with at least one truck getting swept into the river. At least one campground was flooded and evacuated near Marion, while multiple roads were flooded due to small stream flooding throughout western McDowell County, including Cane Creek Rd, Hebron Rd, and Old Fort Sugar Hill Rd. Several mudslides also occurred throughout the area, including one that blocked and closed Interstate 40 near mile marker 67. A number of trees also fell in the saturated soil, including one that fell on a vehicle on Lytle Mountain Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple cars were stalled out due to high water. The water was covering all of the road and yards at some spots and is up to the houses.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 3/4 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed across the Blue Ridge during the afternoon and moved into the Piedmont during the evening. Some of the storms produced severe weather in the form of large hail and locally damaging winds. Strong to severe supercell thunderstorms developed repeatedly and trained across portions of the foothills, particularly in Polk County, such that some locations received multiple periods of up to golf ball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported nickel to quarter size hail off Salem Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wait for it.

Roads impacted by the flooded river included Johns River Rd, Old Johns River Rd, and Johns River Loop Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wait for it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources reported significant flash flooding developed across western Polk County, particularly in the Saluda area after 4 to 7 inches of rain fell across the area in just a few hours. Some of the main impacts were along Cove Creek and its tributaries, where water rescues were required in the area around I-26 and Ozone Dr. Multiple roads in this area, including I-26 itself were flooded due to drainage problems and small streams. A tributary of Cove Creek flooded Howard Gap Rd at Rhododendron Dr. Across the northern part of the county, Lake Adger Parkway was covered with water from Silver Creek. Flooding was also reported along the North Pacolet River along the Highway 176 corridor between Columbus and Saluda. While the flooding was significant, the worst impact of the heavy rain was from numerous landslides and slope failures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wait for it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain ended across western Polk County late in the evening on the 18th, high water conditions persisted across the Saluda and Green River area through the overnight and much of the daylight hours on the 19th before the water finally receded.

An axis of very heavy rainfall pushed north across the Carolinas in the late afternoon and evening as the weak area of low pressure over the southeast lifted slowly northward. Slow-moving storms with highly efficient rainfall affected number of areas in the mountains and foothills of northern North Carolina. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding was reported across southern Watauga County. The Boone Fire Department said a woman driving on a flooded section of Bamboo Road got her SUV swept up by the flood waters. Rescue crews quickly got her to safety with no serious injuries. One foot of water was estimated to be flowing down Seven Devils Road onto Route 105 near Foscoe.  Linville Creek was reported to have flooded over the bridge at Linville Creek Road. The Watauga River at Sugar Grove (SGWN7) crested at 16.15 feet, the highest stage since September, 2004. Several debris flows were reported across the area, blocking roads. Overall damage to road infrastructure was around $1 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain indirectly associated with Subtropical Storm Alberto fell across parts of Eastern NC during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road flooding was reported with water 4 to 5 inches deep. An estimated 3 to 4 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure spinning off the southern New England coast produced very strong northerly winds across portions of northeast North Carolina. The very strong winds downed numerous trees, produced structural damage, and caused power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very strong winds downed trees and caused power outages. Wind gust of 52 knots (60 mph) was measured at Shiloh (1 SW).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure spinning off the southern New England coast produced very strong northerly winds across portions of northeast North Carolina. The very strong winds downed numerous trees, produced structural damage, and caused power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very strong winds downed trees, produced minor structural damage, and caused power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure spinning off the southern New England coast produced very strong northerly winds across portions of northeast North Carolina. The very strong winds downed numerous trees, produced structural damage, and caused power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very strong winds downed trees and caused power outages. Wind gust of 51 knots (59 mph) was measured at ECG.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed across the Blue Ridge during the afternoon and moved into the Piedmont during the evening. Some of the storms produced severe weather in the form of large hail and locally damaging winds. Strong to severe supercell thunderstorms developed repeatedly and trained across portions of the foothills, particularly in Polk County, such that some locations received multiple periods of up to golf ball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported mostly nickel size hail with a few quarter size stones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southeast upslope flow into the Blue Ridge escarpment resulted in development of moderate to heavy rain that began around midnight on the 16th and continued for much of the next 18 hours. Total rainfall of 3 to 6 inches resulted in minor flooding  across portions of Transylvania and Henderson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported and stream gauges indicated flooding developed along the Little River and near the headwaters of the French Broad River after moderate to heavy rain produced widespread 3 to 6 inch rainfall amounts in about 12 hours. The Little River flooded Cascade Lake Rd at Merrill Rd, while the French Broad washed out part of Wilson Rd and also flooded portions of Hannah Ford and Island Ford Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stream gauge and county comms reported flooding developed along Mud Creek after 3 to 6 inches of rain fell across Henderson County in about 18 hours. Impacted roads included an access road at the intersection of S Church St and S Main St and Kanuga Rd at Erkwood Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very wet soil conditions resulting from periods of heavy rainfall that began during the middle part of May and continued through the end of the month, producing more than a foot of rain in many locations caused a tree to fall on a vehicle on Highway 176, which resulted in two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported two broadcast journalists were killed when a tree fell on their vehicle as they were driving on Highway 176. The tree fell due to saturated ground from the more than one foot of rain that fell over western Polk County during the latter half of May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening. Heavy rain from a storm falling over ground that was already saturated from more than two weeks of occasional heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding across western Caldwell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported flash flooding developed along portions of Wilson Creek and near the headwaters of the Johns River after around 2 inches of rain fell in a short period of time on already saturated soils. Multiple roads were reported as flooded and closed throughout the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated slow moving thunderstorms developed across the mountains of southwest North Carolina during the evening. One storm produced localized excessive rainfall in Swain County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported flash flooding developed along small streams in southern Swain County after 2 to 3 inches of rain fell in a short period of time. Deep Gar Rd was reportedly washed out at the confluence of Bowers Creek, Cullasaja Branch, and Jones Creek. Needmore was also flooded by an unknown creek. Multiple small landslides were also reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a weak cold front, a line of thunderstorms developed over the western sections of Virginia and north central North Carolina on the evening of May 10th. As this weak front moved into Eastern North Carolina, an area of thunderstorms impacted especially the northern part of our forecast area.  Some of the storms became severe and produced wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was measured from nickel to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the North Carolina Blue Ridge during the afternoon. A few of the storms produced brief severe weather in the form of damaging winds, mainly across the foothills. Later in the evening, a decaying line of thunderstorms entered western North Carolina from Tennessee. Despite the weakening of the storms, a remnant area of strong winds left behind by the storms produced damage in portions of the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported a tree and power lines blown down on Chimney Rock Rd and a tree down on Highway 108.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west would prompt isolated convection in the mountains. As the front pushed the storms east of the Blue Ridge where higher instabilities existed, the storms became more organized producing damaging winds and large hail.

A cold front approaching from the west would prompt isolated convection in the mountains. As the front pushed the storms east of the Blue Ridge where higher instabilities existed, the storms became more organized producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down power lines in the Forbush area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west would prompt isolated convection in the mountains. As the front pushed the storms east of the Blue Ridge where higher instabilities existed, the storms became more organized producing damaging winds and large hail.

Tropical Cyclone Florence made landfall on the 15th and moved slowly west while weakening through the 16th. Gusty winds resulted in a few trees and power lines falling across portions of western North Carolina, with some trees falling on vehicles and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported a tree fell on a mobile home on Moses Ct in Dallas on the morning of the 16th, killing a 3-month-old boy inside the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon from a hot and humid air mass.  These storms blew several trees down in the North Carolina Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees across the southern part of Rockingham County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Florence made landfall on the 15th and moved slowly west while weakening through the 16th. Widespread rainfall of 4 to 10 inches, with locally higher amounts fell across western North Carolina over a 36-48 hour period, with the highest totals reported along the Blue Ridge escarpment, and in southern and eastern sections of the Charlotte Metro area. Localized flash flooding was reported in these areas, with severe flash flooding reported across Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported severe flash flooding developed across Union County after 7 to 13 inches of rain fell across the county in about 24 hours. Dozens of roads were closed at the height of the event during the afternoon of the 16th, some due to poor drainage flooding in urban areas, and others due to small stream flooding, including Goose Creek in the Indian Trail area, where a stream gauge exceeded its established flood stage. Multiple water rescues were necessary, and some homes were isolated by flood waters. A one-year-old boy drowned when his parent drove a vehicle around a barricade and into a swollen creek on Highway 218.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Florence made landfall on the 15th and moved slowly west while weakening through the 16th. Localized flash flooding was reported in these areas, with severe flash flooding reported across Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After 4 to 6 inches of rain fell throughout the 16th, county comms reported numerous roads flooded and multiple roads closed due to flooding, especially across western sections of Rowan County, including Salisbury and vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorm near a stationary boundary produced a weak tornado across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public sited a tornado and tree damage was reported off Winston Farm Road off of Route 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of Yadkin and Wilkes counties during the late afternoon and evening of July 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer measured 5.10 inches for 24-hours ending at 7 AM EDT. This is roughly a 10-year event (0.1 annual exceedance probability).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of Yadkin and Wilkes counties during the late afternoon and evening of July 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yadkinville 6 E COOP station (YKNN7) measured 2.93 for the 24-hours ending at 700 AM EDT on August 1st, the highest recorded on this date at this site since records began there in 1940 (with a break in data from 1952-1957).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Florence made landfall on the far southeast North Carolina coast on September 14th and tracked very slowly across South Carolina before slowly re-curving across far western North Carolina and southwest Virginia as a Tropical Depression and on into the Ohio Valley. Multiple incidents of strong winds, flash flooding and flooding occurred with Florence. The first rainfall of any significance was during the late afternoon and evening of the 14th as several outer bands of Florence affected mainly Rockingham and Caswell counties with 24-hour totals ending 12z (700 AM) on the 15th of 1 to 2 inches, with much lower amounts to the northwest.  The next 24 hours (ending 12z on the 16th) saw heavier rains still mainly over the NC Piedmont counties with about 1 to 3 inches falling. Much heavier rainfall began to spread further northward during the afternoon and evening of the 16th prompting numerous Flash Flood Warnings and causing significant flood impacts.  The highest observed rain amounts in North Carolina within the HSA were over parts of Alleghany County with up to 8 inches in 24 hours (ending at 700 AM on the 17th) and over 10 inches storm total. The highest gage report was from Glade Creek VFD DCP (GLCN7) with 10.99 inches in the 72-hour period ending 700 AM on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Sparta (SPRN7) measured 7.64 inches for the 24-hours ending 700 AM local time on September 17th. This was the highest daily rainfall for any date on record at this station. The old record was 7.10 inches August 31, 1964. This value falls into the 90 percent confidence interval (7.02-8.57 inches) for a 50-year average recurrence interval (.02 annual exceedance probability) per NOAA Atlas 2. Continuous precipitation data began in 1942 and ended in mid-2010 but resumed in mid-2018 at this station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One severe thunderstorm developed during the afternoon and became severe over the North Carolina Piedmont.  This storm blew a few trees down in Stokes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down multiple trees on Lynchburg Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One severe thunderstorm developed during the afternoon and became severe over the North Carolina Piedmont.  This storm blew a few trees down in Stokes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down multiple trees on Snow Hill Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving front with a deep upper trough pulling moisture into the region fueled pockets of heavy rainfall in the early to mid-morning. A few storms became severe by afternoon blowing down trees in several counties. Heavy rainfall redeveloped by mid- to late afternoon as a complex, broken line of convection pushed north under deep, moist southerly flow. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 3 inches in a wide swath across Rockingham and parts of Surry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down multiple trees in Pleasant Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low combined with a passing cold front sparked a severe thunderstorm during the afternoon.  This storm blew down several trees in Wilkes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down one tree along John McGrady Road.

Small stream flooding was also reported including Flat Shoal Creek near Sunset Park campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front produced strong nocturnal storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down on Castle St, and 18th St.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trailing band of heavy rain moved across the piedmont during the early to mid-morning hours of the 17th and produced a broad swath of 2 to 4 inches of rain in several hours across central Caswell County. A CoCoRaHS site 2.4 SW of Yanceyville (NC-CS-5) had a storm total of 4.97 inches ending at 700 AM local time on the 18th, much of which fell in during this several-hour period. Dispatch and emergency management reported numerous road closures across the county including portions of Walters Mill Road, Route 62, Route 119, Route 158 East, Jack Pointer Road, Race Track Road and Doll Branch Road. The spatial extent and duration of flooding was partially delineated by unit streamflow from the CREST hydrologic model.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving front with a deep upper trough pulling moisture into the region fueled pockets of heavy rainfall in the early to mid-morning. A few storms became severe by afternoon blowing down trees in several counties. Heavy rainfall redeveloped by mid- to late afternoon as a complex, broken line of convection pushed north under deep, moist southerly flow. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 3 inches in a wide swath across Rockingham and parts of Surry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were reported throughout Rockingham County due to heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches in several hours. This followed heavy rains the day before as well. At least two roads were severely damaged as culverts were washed out and sinkholes formed. The most damage occurred on Webb Road where pipes under the road collapsed and the road was swept away. A tobacco farm nearby suffered flood damages with approximately $100,000 in losses. A private road was damaged cutting off access for nine persons living along Sunrise Acres Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reidsville 2 NW COOP station (RDVN7) measured 2.67 inches for the 24-hour period ending at 700 AM EDT on the 3rd. This was the highest ever for this date and the 8th highest August daily rainfall amount, with data back to 1962.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms producing gusty winds and heavy rainfall developed over the North Carolina Piedmont and foothills during the late afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Florence made landfall on the far southeast North Carolina coast on September 14th and tracked very slowly across South Carolina before slowly re-curving across far western North Carolina and southwest Virginia as a Tropical Depression and on into the Ohio Valley. Multiple incidents of strong winds, flash flooding and flooding occurred with Florence. The first rainfall of any significance was during the late afternoon and evening of the 14th as several outer bands of Florence affected mainly Rockingham and Caswell counties with 24-hour totals ending 12z (700 AM) on the 15th of 1 to 2 inches, with much lower amounts to the northwest.  The next 24 hours (ending 12z on the 16th) saw heavier rains still mainly over the NC Piedmont counties with about 1 to 3 inches falling. Much heavier rainfall began to spread further northward during the afternoon and evening of the 16th prompting numerous Flash Flood Warnings and causing significant flood impacts.  The highest observed rain amounts in North Carolina within the HSA were over parts of Alleghany County with up to 8 inches in 24 hours (ending at 700 AM on the 17th) and over 10 inches storm total. The highest gage report was from Glade Creek VFD DCP (GLCN7) with 10.99 inches in the 72-hour period ending 700 AM on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred across Yadkin County with at least a half-dozen roads closed as of early Monday morning, the 17th.  These included Dinkins Bottom Road, Butner Mill Road, South Jonesville Boulevard, Richmond Hill Road at the Rockford bridge, Aquila Creek Road and Old 421 East in the Forbush area remained closed to traffic due to flooding on the roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork of the New River near Jefferson (JFRN7) crested at 9.43 feet (Minor flood stage is 8 ft.) in the early morning of the 17th. Several roads are affected at this stage. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain showers and thunderstorms produced the second flash flood event in less than 24 hours along the North Fork Catawba basin when up to 3 inches of rain fell on already saturated ground within a couple of hours. Impacts were similar, only slightly worse than the event that occurred during late evening of the 20th, with multiple tributaries of the North Fork, most notably Pond Branch and Dogback Spring Branch, overflowing their banks and flooding roads, including portions of Highway 221. Additionally, the same structures along Blue Ridge Dr that were flooded late on the 20th were again flooded during this time, with up to a foot of water entering first floors. A stream gauge on the North Fork just upstream of Ashford also exceeded its established flood stage of 9 feet, indicating flooding of a campground on Highway 221. Several new small land slides developed as well, especially along Blue Ridge Dr.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported a large tree was blown down on a vehicle in Green Mountain Park killing a 78-year-old occupant and causing minor injuries to two others. A few other trees were blown down in Lenoir and areas just east of the city.

This was the 6th highest stage on record at this gage with over 65 years of data. According to USGS analysis, the flood frequency for the event was between a 5- and 10-year (0.2 to 0.1 annual chance of occurrence). Parts of Mayo Beach Road near the river were flooded.

This was the 8th highest stage on record at this gage since records began in 1940 and the highest since 2003. According to USGS analysis, the frequency for the event was approximately a 10-year flood (0.1 annual chance of occurrence). Several roads were flooded and closed near the confluence of the Mayo and Dan rivers in the Madison area per local news reports.

Some of these storms became severe and produced wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front propelled by an deep upper trough that took on a negative tilt pushed through on April 15 producing severe weather and heavy rainfall. The speed of convective elements prevented any flash flooding by longer duration flooding occurred on several rivers and streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Jefferson 2E (JEFN7) measured 3.20 inches of rain for the 24-hour period ending 7AM on the 16th. This was the 3rd highest 1-day April rainfall total in the period of record at this location, with data starting in 1896.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Watauga River at Sugar Grove (SGWN7) rose very rapidly,  cresting at 10.39 feet, well above the Minor Flood Stage of 6 feet. Several low water bridges were flooded roads near the river were also flooded and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meso-scale boundary along the North Carolina-Virginia state line sparked a complex of slow moving convection that produced 4 to 5 inches of rain and isolated flash flooding across Person County. This complex then moved southward into the Triangle region around midnight, where it produced another 3 to 4 inch swath of rain over the Raleigh-Durham Metro area. This heavy rainfall led to numerous reports of flash flooding within the Raleigh urban area, with multiple water rescues. Additionally, with saturated soils, the thunderstorms also produced scattered wind damage across the Raleigh metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of flooded roadways in Bethel Hill, including Bethel Hill School Road, Gentry-Massey Road, Bowmantown Road, and John Obie Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet period developed from April 23-25 as an upper level low over the southeastern U.S. slowly tracked toward the Atlantic coast. Rainfall was persistent during this period with sustained south to southeasterly flow with the heaviest rains falling in the 24-hour period ending 12z (7AM) on the 24th with 3 to 4+ inch amounts common. The highest totals occurred over Watauga County, NC. Mainly minor flooding was observed in several areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Watauga River at Sugar Grove (SGWN7) crested at 7.32 feet, just over the Minor flood stage of 6 feet. Several low water bridges and roads near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed over central NC in association with a series of disturbances moving through the long wave trough aloft. Deep moist advection and unidirectional flow through the atmosphere resulted in training of heavy rain and thunderstorms. Saturated antecedent conditions contributed to the flash flooding that occurred as a result of the training storms. A cluster of storms became severe during the afternoon, resulting in sporadic downed trees and power lines while other marginally severe storms resulted in numerous downed trees and power lines due to saturated ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Welcome-Arcadia Road and Cecil Road was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with a negatively-tilted upper level trough approached the North Carolina Piedmont. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed over central NC in association with a series of disturbances moving through the long wave trough aloft. Deep moist advection and unidirectional flow through the atmosphere resulted in training of heavy rain and thunderstorms. Saturated antecedent conditions contributed to the flash flooding that occurred as a result of the training storms. A cluster of storms became severe during the afternoon, resulting in sporadic downed trees and power lines while other marginally severe storms resulted in numerous downed trees and power lines due to saturated ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets were closed due to flash flooding throughout the city of Durham, including sections of Roxboro Street and Lumley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with a negatively-tilted upper level trough approached the North Carolina Piedmont.  Plenty of wind shear with modest instability made conditions ripe for large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes.  A supercell ahead of the cold front became strong enough to produce two tornadoes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Caswell County west of Pelham near Dusty Lane at 4:51 PM EST.  It snapped numerous trees and damaged several outbuildings. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain showers and embedded thunderstorms developed across the North Carolina Piedmont during the afternoon and evening. Flash flooding developed in the Statesville area after 2.5 to 4 inches of rain fell in just a couple of hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms and others reported flash flooding developed on the east side of Statesville and adjacent areas after 2.5 to 4 inches of rain fell across a large area, mostly within a couple of hours. There were multiple reports of creeks and small streams overflowing their banks and flooding roads, with Beaver Creek and its tributaries being the especially problematic. One to two feet of water from a tributary was flowing over Old Mocksville Rd near Davis Regional Med Center. A retaining pond on Beaver Creek near Old Mocksville Rd was spilling over the dam. Part of Old Mocksville Rd was washed away near Moore Ridge Rd due to flooding along Fifth Creek. Urban flooding was also reported, with water entering basements on Broad St. Finally, dozens of campers were trapped by flood water at a campground on Log Rd southeast of Statesville when tributaries of Third Creek washed out part of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet period developed from April 23-25 as an upper level low over the southeastern U.S. slowly tracked toward the Atlantic coast. Rainfall was persistent during this period with sustained south to southeasterly flow with the heaviest rains falling in the 24-hour period ending 12z (7AM) on the 24th with 3 to 4+ inch amounts common. The highest totals occurred over Watauga County, NC. Mainly minor flooding was observed in several areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Boone 1 SE (BOON7) measured 3.94 inches for the 24-hour period ending 7AM on the 24th. This was the highest 1-day April rainfall in the period of record at this location, with data starting in 1980. The previous April record at this site was set less than 10 days earlier on April 16, 2018. If the previous COOP site at Boone is included in the comparison (with data back to 1929) this was the 2nd wettest April day after April 5, 1957 with 4.68 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to widely scattered thunderstorms developed across western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening. Several storms organized into a small cluster and produced brief damaging winds in Cleveland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass sparked one severe thunderstorm during the evening.  This storm blew down three trees in Rockingham County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass sparked one severe thunderstorm during the evening.  This storm blew down three trees in Rockingham County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with a negatively-tilted upper level trough approached the North Carolina Piedmont.  Plenty of wind shear with modest instability made conditions ripe for large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes.  A supercell ahead of the cold front became strong enough to produce two tornadoes.  The first tornado initially formed in Guilford County but crossed into Rockingham County and caused noteworthy damage, while a second tornado touched down in Caswell County and continued into Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail varying from the size of quarters to half dollar coins fell for several minutes and covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent plume of tropical moisture and embedded perturbations aloft triggered clusters of convection with torrential downpours. Since the wind field was fairly unidirectional, training cells lead to locally excessive rainfall and flash flooding, along with isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Highway 109.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper low closed off over eastern Ohio, with high precipitable water values over central and eastern North Carolina with improving deep layer shear and 1500-3000 J/kg of MLCAPE fueled numerous storms and storm clusters from early afternoon through the evening. Locally heavy downpours resulted in isolated flash flooding and isolated strong to severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down near Old Davis Road and Gaddys Ferry Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave energy associated with the exiting closed core upper low over the northern Mid Atlantic states pinwheeled and spurred convection along a thermal moisture boundary that extended southward near United States Highway 64. The convection moved east and intensified as they encountered the better shear and instability. Isolated wind damage occurred from one of the storms across Nash County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Beulah Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of showers and thunderstorms developed and became anchored along the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge during the evening. Five to six inches of rain fell along the border of northern McDowell and Burke Counties, resulting in flash flooding along tributaries of the North Fork Catawba River in McDowell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported flash flooding developed along several small tributaries to the North Fork Catawba River after as much as six inches of rain fell in the basin in just a couple of hours. A small tributary overflowed its banks along Blue Ridge Dr, with water entering the first floors of several structures. Another tributary eroded the sublayer beneath Blue Ridge Dr South, causing the road to be closed. Multiple small mudslides were also reported along the Highway 221 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain along with embedded thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a stationary front across western North Carolina throughout the afternoon and evening. Widespread rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches produced localized flooding across northern McDowell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM and public (via Social Media) reported flooding developed along a couple of tributaries of the North Fork Catawba River after 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across the basin throughout the afternoon into the evening. The main creeks affected were Armstrong Creek and Martin Branch, both of which overflowed onto adjacent roads, including Highway 221, where a swift water rescue became necessary.

There were frequent CG strikes that affected Harnett, Johnston, and eastern Wake Counties as well, with some house fires reported.

While severe weather was confined to the mountains on the 23rd and 24th, storms on the 25th also produced severe weather in the foothills and northwest Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported numerous trees and power lines blown down in western Burke County. Media reported numerous trees down in the Morganton area, especially along the Catawba River. At least one road was closed in Morganton due to falling trees. Additional sporadic tree damage was also reported outside of this corridor, including at Highway 18 and Antioch Rd near the Caldwell County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man drowned in an apparent rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A male visiting from Beaverdam, Virginia drowned in a rip current near the Atlantic Beach circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection blossomed strongly near the intersection of a southward moving outflow boundary and a similar eastward moving boundary. The convection moved east along the boundary, with one cluster of storms becoming severe, producing a swath of wind damage from Orange county to Wake county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down along a swath from the Shinleaf Campground at Falls Lake to Old Highway 98.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed along a cold front moving eastward through the region during the early evening. One cell produced isolated wind damage as it moved through Stanly county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down along a swath from Richfield to Newsom. Dime sized hail was also reported near the intersection of Highway 49 and Ingram Road in Richfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level trough drifting across the central Appalachians and southern middle Atlantic states resulted in scattered convection development and outflow mergers across central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down on Polly Watson Road in Fremont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few multi-cell clusters developed ahead of a well-defined, convectively enhanced vorticity maximum that crossed the mountains and moved into the area during the late afternoon and evening. Two of the clusters produced minor wind damage in the form of scattered trees down in Forsyth and Chatham counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down along a swath from mile marker 247 on US-421 to the intersection of Ridge Gate Drive and Arbor Run Drive in Lewisville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from previous night's convection was stalled over the southeast third of NC, roughly from Camp Lejeune to Florence, SC. Meanwhile a surface cold front was drifting southward out of southern VA into NC. Thunderstorms erupted along this outflow by early afternoon. Outflows from these storms caused others to develop. The stronger cells produced quarter size hail along with isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail was reported at  7071 U.S. Highway 421, Mamers, NC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in 10 days, a freezing rain event developed across portions of the mountains and foothills of North Carolina. Precipitation developed during the overnight, as a wave of low pressure moved along the Gulf Coast. Precipitation began as rain and snow, but transitioned to freezing rain across all but the highest elevations (where more of a wintry mix was reported), as a wedge of cold air locked in near the Blue Ridge. As freezing rain continued through daybreak, damaging accumulations of ice, generally between one quarter and ine half inch were reported across the area. Quite a few trees and power lines, along with numerous power outages were reported across the area.
A cold front produced strong thunderstorms with small to medium size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported at the Magnolia Greens golf course.

Two thunderstorm clusters produced some scattered wind damage. A back-ward building cluster produced heavy rainfall of 3 to 4 inches that resulted in isolated flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough with a slow-moving frontal boundary extending from Texas to upstate New York brought an extended period of mainly light to moderate rainfall that began on the afternoon of the 10th and persisted well into the evening of the 11th. Storm total rainfall amounts ranged from 1.5 to 3 inches across parts of northwest North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at W. Kerr Scott Dam had a 24-hour total of 3.08 inches for the 24-hour period ending at 7 AM on the 11th. This was the 4th highest daily rainfall total during the month of February at this location since records began in 1965.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep Gulf of Mexico moisture channeling between an amplifying trough over the mid-lower MS Valley and the western Atlantic ridge led to precipitable waters increasing to to over 2.0 over the area. Upper forcing in the form of divergence aloft, within the right entrance region of an upper level jet streak that extended down the Mid-Atlantic States, combined with a south-north ribbon of elongated vorticity shear axis centered east of the southern and central Appalachians, led to the development of scattered to numerous thunderstorms through the late afternoon and evening. The heaviest rain fell over the tri-county area of Nash-Wilson-Edgecombe counties, where training thunderstorms produced a swath of 4 to 6 inches.  This area of heavy rainfall flooded numerous roads in these counties, with at least two water rescues in Nash County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intersection of South Old Carriage Road and Baywood Road closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a moisture-rich and hot Summertime air mass and triggered showers and storms. One of these storms increased to severe levels, producing damaging winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along NC Highway 55, near the intersection of Carson Gregory Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms initiated in split upper level flow along a prefrontal trough. Mean 850 MB to 300 MB winds were near calm at the time with storm propagation mainly cold pool driven. The primary impacts from these storms were gusty winds, resulting in trees down, and heavy rain. One storm did become anchored over Raleigh which prompted a flash flood warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The leading edge of cool air nosing down the coast caused widespread, mainly stratiform rain which was slow moving and producing rainfall rates greater than one inch per hour for several hours. The front nearly stalled across Wake County during the morning, with a heavy rain focus south of Raleigh down into northern Harnett county. Radar estimated fairly extensive 2 to 3.35 inches of rainfall. This resulted in a couple of flash flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Banks Road and Ten Ten Road were reported to be flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow-augmented back-door cold front, served as the foci for a slow northeast to southwest moving cluster of storms and torrential rainfall across the northern Piedmont and northern Coastal Plain counties. The heaviest rainfall of 3 to 5 inches fell across North Raleigh, which caused Crabtree Creek to flood. Multiple road closures were reported in northwest Raleigh and several cars were stranded in a flooded road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed in northwest Raleigh, many in the vicinity of Crabtree Valley Mall and Crabtree Creek, due to flash flooding. Newton Road near Six Forks Road washed out and collapsed. Several cars were stranded in flood water near the intersection of Leesville Road and Millbrook Road. Several cars were underwater at the Crabtree Valley Mall due to flooding along Crabtree Creek overflowing its banks into the parking garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep upper trough for July over the eastern U.S. extending all the way south to the Gulf Coast provided the setting for widespread shower and thunderstorm activity. Radar estimated rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in about 3 hours was observed across west central Rockingham County with a small area estimated at 4 to 6 inches between Intelligence and Mayodan. The annual recurrence interval of the heaviest rain approached 200-year (.005 annual exceedance probability) in a 3-hour period per NOAA Atlas 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS station at Eden 2.3 ENE measured 3.84 inches for the 24-hours ending 7 AM on July 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge off the east coast and an upper low over the central U.S. combined to bring enhanced moisture into the region. Weak low pressure along a frontal boundary draped near the Virginia-North Carolina border provided additional convergence and lift to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms mainly over the piedmont and Blue Ridge foothills. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches across parts of northwest Wilkes, southeast Alleghany, western Surry, and eastern Yadkin counties, most of which fell in about a 3-hour period during the late evening hours of July 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The W. Kerr Scott Reservoir COOP station (WLKN7) measured 2.98 inches for the 24-hours ending at 7 AM on the 31st. This was the highest amount at this station for July 31st and 2nd highest for any July day, behind the 3.33��� that fell on July 16, 1978. Station records began in July 1965.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge off the east coast and an upper low over the central U.S. combined to bring enhanced moisture into the region. Weak low pressure along a frontal boundary draped near the Virginia-North Carolina border provided additional convergence and lift to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms mainly over the piedmont and Blue Ridge foothills. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches across parts of northwest Wilkes, southeast Alleghany, western Surry, and eastern Yadkin counties, most of which fell in about a 3-hour period during the late evening hours of July 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of flash flooding and flooding in western Wilkes County. The East Prong of the Roaring River flooded Twin Locust and Grissel Trail Roads with 5 to 6 feet of water flowing across them. Flooding was also reported along Little Sandy Creek which flooded Sandy Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary surface low over western North Carolina, and a pair of outflow modulated fronts over North Carolina and Virginia, were overspread by perturbed south southwesterly flow around an anomalous mid to upper low over the Ohio Valley. Loosely organized multi clusters resulted, with accompanying marginally severe hail, wind damage, and isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Swift Creek School Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms initiated in split upper level flow along a prefrontal trough. Mean 850 MB to 300 MB winds were near calm at the time with storm propagation mainly cold pool driven. The primary impacts from these storms were gusty winds, resulting in trees down, and heavy rain. One storm did become anchored over Raleigh which prompted a flash flood warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep Gulf of Mexico moisture channeling between an amplifying trough over the mid-lower MS Valley and the western Atlantic ridge led to precipitable waters increasing to to over 2.0 over the area. Upper forcing in the form of divergence aloft, within the right entrance region of an upper level jet streak that extended down the Mid-Atlantic States, combined with a south-north ribbon of elongated vorticity shear axis centered east of the southern and central Appalachians, led to the development of scattered to numerous thunderstorms through the late afternoon and evening. The heaviest rain fell over the tri-county area of Nash-Wilson-Edgecombe counties, where training thunderstorms produced a swath of 4 to 6 inches.  This area of heavy rainfall flooded numerous roads in these counties, with at least two water rescues in Nash County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 4 to 6 inches resulted in widespread flash flooding across the city of Wilson and northern portions of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chapel Hill Road was closed near the intersection of North Carolina Highway 54 due to flooding.

Airy had a 20-foot opening in the road where rising waters destroyed a section of the road. The automobile was trapped in the high water although the driver escaped without injury. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with a negatively-tilted upper level trough approached the North Carolina Piedmont.  Plenty of wind shear with modest instability made conditions ripe for large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes.  A supercell ahead of the cold front became strong enough to produce two tornadoes.  The first tornado initially formed in Guilford County but crossed into Rockingham County and caused noteworthy damage, while a second tornado touched down in Caswell County and continued into Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down southeast of Greensboro in Guilford County shortly after 4:00 PM EST.  This tornado later entered Rockingham County about one mile southeast of Haw River near Chrismon Road at 4:25 PM EST.  The tornado damaged numerous buildings, outbuildings, and barns.  At least twenty homes and farms suffered damage, while one house and two mobile homes were  completely destroyed.  The Oak Haven Event Center in downtown Ruffin also sustained damage.  Countless trees were snapped or uprooted.  Estimated maximum wind speeds were 110 MPH.  Seven people were injured, including a father and a seven-year-old son who were critically injured in their moving car when it struck a double-wide mobile home that was lifted on to the 1300 block of Grooms Road.  The tornado finally lifted at 4:46 PM EST about four miles north-northeast of Ruffin between Dibrell Road and Bradley Road.

Storms produced a massive outflow boundary and excessive amounts of lightning. Storm coverage was more limited to the south and east in the Coastal Plain and southern Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yanceyville Street was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep upper trough for July over the eastern U.S. extending all the way south to the Gulf Coast provided the setting for widespread shower and thunderstorm activity. Radar estimated rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in about 3 hours was observed across west central Rockingham County with a small area estimated at 4 to 6 inches between Intelligence and Mayodan. The annual recurrence interval of the heaviest rain approached 200-year (.005 annual exceedance probability) in a 3-hour period per NOAA Atlas 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sinkhole formed and the road was badly damaged along North Carolina Route 135/311 where heavy rain occurred and runoff flowed across the road. The flooding was aggravated by a water main break under the road. Damage estimate per NC DOT. A water rescue also occurred where one person was stranded in a vehicle that was halfway submerged near the intersection of MacGregor Lane and River Road.

The primary impacts from these storms were gusty winds, resulting in trees down, and heavy rain. One storm did become anchored over Raleigh which prompted a flash flood warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down across the road near the intersection of Lindsey Street and Salisbury Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front, bisecting central NC, provided a focus point for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. Thanks to a moist and unstable airmass, a few of these storms breached the severe limit, resulting in isolated wind damage, and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees onto roadways across the eastern half of the county. These trees primarily came down on Hood Swamp Road and Parkerstown Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking northeast just off the southeast and Mid Atlantic coasts produced snowfall totals between two inches and seven inches across portions of interior northeast North Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple disturbances rotating around an upper-level low across the Ohio Valley produced multiple clusters of showers and storms across central NC during the late evening and  overnight hours. Convection congealed into an MCS-like complex and pushed east during the overnight hours which produced some isolated wind damage and pockets of heavy rainfall which produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down near NC Highway 42.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front propelled by an deep upper trough that took on a negative tilt pushed through on April 15 producing severe weather and heavy rainfall. The speed of convective elements prevented any flash flooding by longer duration flooding occurred on several rivers and streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Transou (LSPN7) measured 3.44 inches for the 24-hour period ending 7AM on the 16th. This was the 6th highest 1-day April rainfall total in the period of record at this location, with data starting in 1946.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over eastern North America stalled Florence's forward motion a few miles off the southeast North Carolina coast on September 13th. Hurricane Florence made landfall near Wrightsville Beach early on Saturday September 15, and weakened further as it moved slowly inland.  Despite making landfall as a weakened|Category 1 hurricane, Florence still produced 40 to 70 mph wind gusts, enough wind speed to uproot trees and cause widespread power outages throughout the Carolinas.  As the storm moved inland, from September 15 to 17,  heavy rain of 10 to 25 inches  caused widespread inland flooding, inundating cities such as Fayetteville, Smithfield, Goldsboro, Durham, and Chapel Hill, and causing major river flooding on main-stem rivers such as the Neuse, Cape Fear, and Little River. Most major roads and highways in the area experienced some flooding, with large stretches of I-40 and I-95 remaining impassable for days after the storm had passed. The storm also spawned tornadoes in several places along its path. There were 3 direct and 6 indirect deaths attributed to the storm with in the WFO RAH CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall of 15 to 20 inches caused widespread flooding across the county and throughout the Lumber River basin. Flooding damaged approximately 168 structures throughout the county, resulting in over $5.4 million in property damage and at least $30 million in crop damage. Numerous roads were flooded all throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the high elevations of the North Carolina mountains as freezing levels quickly fell in the wake of a strong cold front. A cold and moist northwest flow developing in the wake of the front across the Tennessee Valley and the southern Appalachians resulted in scattered to numerous snow showers continuing near the Tennessee border throughout the afternoon and evening of the 21st, through the morning of the 22nd. Accumulations were mainly confined to elevations above 3500 feet, where 1 to 3 inches generally fell. Some locations above 5000 feet reported as much as a half foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of rain and snow developed across the mountains and southern foothills of North Carolina during the afternoon and evening of the 8th, transitioning to all snow in most areas by early evening. Hurricane Florence made landfall near Wrightsville Beach early on Saturday September 15, and weakened further as it moved slowly inland.  Despite making landfall as a weakened|Category 1 hurricane, Florence still produced 40 to 70 mph wind gusts, enough wind speed to uproot trees and cause widespread power outages throughout the Carolinas.  As the storm moved inland, from September 15 to 17,  heavy rain of 10 to 25 inches  caused widespread inland flooding, inundating cities such as Fayetteville, Smithfield, Goldsboro, Durham, and Chapel Hill, and causing major river flooding on main-stem rivers such as the Neuse, Cape Fear, and Little River. Most major roads and highways in the area experienced some flooding, with large stretches of I-40 and I-95 remaining impassable for days after the storm had passed. The storm also spawned tornadoes in several places along its path. There were 3 direct and 6 indirect deaths attributed to the storm with in the WFO RAH CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 6 to 8 inches caused widespread flooding across the county. Combined with additional rainfall upstream, the rainfall caused all-time record major flooding along the Rocky River near Norwood. Flooding damaged approximately 142 structures throughout the county, destroying 3 and resulting in over $3.63 million in property damage and at least $20 million in crop damage. Numerous roads were flooded all throughout the county. The Rocky River overflowed the Highway 52 bridge and reached the base of the Plank Road bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third late December significant rainfall event developed after Christmas as the active southern jet stream helped to spin up another complex area of low pressure which slowly moved through the southeast and Mid-Atlantic December 27-29. Rainfall over the 2-day  was approximately 1 to 3 inches across the region with isolated higher amounts up to 3.5 inches.  Rain gage reports of 3 inches or more in the NC counties for the 48-hour period ending 700 AM local time on the 29th included Blackburn Knob IFLOWS (BNKN7) 3.67 inches, Sandy Flats IFLOWS (SDRN7) 3.56 inches, Boone RG (BONN7) 3.45 inches,  Creek at Elkville DCP (EKVN7) 3.16 inches, and W. Kerr Scott Reservoir (WLKN7) 3.00 inches. Portions of the Dan River in NC rose above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Elkin (EKNN7) reported 1.95 inches for the 24-hours ending at 700 AM local time, which was the second highest on record for December 29th. The record is 2.31 inches in 1915. Continuous climate records at this station exist since 1913.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain developed across western North Carolina on the 27th in advance of a strong cold front. The rain persisted through much of the 28th before tapering off late in the day. Storm total rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches occurred across the mountains, with locally higher amounts in excess of 7 inches. This resulted in flooding of the French Broad River and some of its larger tributaries, as well as on a few other streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stream gauges along the Swannanoa River in Buncombe County exceeded their established flood stages after 3 to 4 inches of rain fell throughout the basin in about 24 hours. Low spots on the campus of Warren Wilson College were inundated. The primary impacts were in Biltmore Village in Asheville. The river, along with backwater effects into smaller tributaries flooded and closed multiple streets, including Garfield St, Decatur St, Caledonia Rd, Glendale Ave, Swannanoa River Rd, and Thompson St. Water entered the lower levels of a couple of businesses on Decatur St and Garfield St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of rain and snow developed across the North Carolina mountains during the afternoon of the 8th, transitioning to all snow in most areas by early evening. The snow became heavy at times during the evening into the overnight. By the time the snow tapered off during the morning of the 9th, widespread storm total accumulations of 10-15 inches were reported. Locally higher amounts also occurred, with some locations near the South Carolina border seeing around 20 inches. Meanwhile, warm air filtering into the lower elevations of the Tuckasegee River Valley resulted in a transition to rain during the morning of the 9th, and total accumulations there were only in the 2 to 4 inch range.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county ranged from a half inch across southern portions to 4 inches across the north. A thin glaze of ice from freezing rain was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third late December significant rainfall event developed after Christmas as the active southern jet stream helped to spin up another complex area of low pressure which slowly moved through the southeast and Mid-Atlantic December 27-29. Rainfall over the 2-day  was approximately 1 to 3 inches across the region with isolated higher amounts up to 3.5 inches.  Rain gage reports of 3 inches or more in the NC counties for the 48-hour period ending 700 AM local time on the 29th included Blackburn Knob IFLOWS (BNKN7) 3.67 inches, Sandy Flats IFLOWS (SDRN7) 3.56 inches, Boone RG (BONN7) 3.45 inches,  Creek at Elkville DCP (EKVN7) 3.16 inches, and W. Kerr Scott Reservoir (WLKN7) 3.00 inches. Portions of the Dan River in NC rose above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at W. Kerr Scott Reservoir (WLKN7) reported 1.84 inches for the 24-hours ending at 700 AM local time, which was the highest on record for December 29th. The old record was 1.59 inches in 2015. Continuous climate records at this station exist since 1965.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over eastern North America stalled Florence's forward motion a few miles off the southeast North Carolina coast on September 13th. Hurricane Florence made landfall near Wrightsville Beach early on Saturday September 15, and weakened further as it moved slowly inland.  Despite making landfall as a weakened|Category 1 hurricane, Florence still produced 40 to 70 mph wind gusts, enough wind speed to uproot trees and cause widespread power outages throughout the Carolinas.  As the storm moved inland, from September 15 to 17,  heavy rain of 10 to 25 inches  caused widespread inland flooding, inundating cities such as Fayetteville, Smithfield, Goldsboro, Durham, and Chapel Hill, and causing major river flooding on main-stem rivers such as the Neuse, Cape Fear, and Little River. Most major roads and highways in the area experienced some flooding, with large stretches of I-40 and I-95 remaining impassable for days after the storm had passed. The storm also spawned tornadoes in several places along its path. There were 3 direct and 6 indirect deaths attributed to the storm with in the WFO RAH CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph resulted in numerous trees down across Edgecombe County, including on homes, cars, power lines and damage to structures. Numerous customers lost power in Edgecombe County as a result of the tropical storm force winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of rain and snow developed across the North Carolina mountains during the afternoon of the 8th, transitioning to all snow in most areas by early evening. The snow became heavy at times during the evening into the overnight. By the time the snow tapered off during the morning of the 9th, widespread storm total accumulations of 10-15 inches were reported. Locally higher amounts also occurred, with some locations near the South Carolina border seeing around 20 inches. Meanwhile, warm air filtering into the lower elevations of the Tuckasegee River Valley resulted in a transition to rain during the morning of the 9th, and total accumulations there were only in the 2 to 4 inch range.

A low pressure system tracked eastward along the Gulf Coast and spread moisture northward toward the Mid Atlantic.  Meanwhile, high pressure to the north allowed temperatures to fall into the 20s, which caused the moisture to mostly fall as heavy snow.  Some sleet and freezing drizzle also fell during this storm.  The heavy snow caused numerous vehicle accidents and downed trees that fell on to roads and power lines, and it also caused one indirect fatality in Yadkin County.  Average snowfall accumulations ranged from ten to twenty inches over northwest North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 16.0 inches about four miles south-southwest of Sparta to 19.0 inches about two miles south-southeast of Whitehead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain developed across western North Carolina in association with a slow-moving warm front on the 20th and continued into the 21st. Localized minor flooding developed on a couple of streams in the Piedmont and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on the Rocky River near Irish Buffalo Creek exceeded its established flood stage after widespread rainfall of around 2 inches fell within the basin in about 24 hours. Multiple tributaries of the river overflowed and flooded roads, including Pharr Mill Rd and Stallings Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain developed across western North Carolina on the 27th in advance of a strong cold front. The rain persisted through much of the 28th before tapering off late in the day. Storm total rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches occurred across the mountains, with locally higher amounts in excess of 7 inches. This resulted in flooding of the French Broad River and some of its larger tributaries, as well as on a few other streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on the Johns River north of Morganton exceeded its established flood stage after around 3 inches of rain fell throughout the basin in about a 24 hour period. Flooded roads included Johns River Loop Rd, Old Johns River Rd, and Valley View Cir. In addition, Warrior Fork overflowed its banks and flooded the intersection of Spainhour Rd and Bost Rd, while Bristol Creek flooded part of Highway 64 in the Chesterfield community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another southern stream dominated system emerged from the southwestern U.S. with a complex surface low tracking from the Gulf Coast into the southern Mid-Atlantic December 20-22. This was again an all liquid event, with 2-day rainfall totals (ending 12z, or 7 AM EST, on 12/22) ranging from 1 to 4 inches with isolated higher amounts mainly along and near the Blue Ridge. The top five rainfall reports in northwest North Carolina in the 48-hours ending 700 AM local time on the 22nd included Boone RG (BONN7) 4.62 inches, Glade Creek VFD DCP (GLCN7) 4.54 inches, Sandy Flats IFLOWS (SDRN7) 4.24 inches, Sparta 3.5 SSW CCR (NC-AG-1) 4.19 inches and Blowing Rock 4.4 NE CCR (NC-WT-6) 3.93 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NC State Highway 770 was closed due to flooding near Buffalo Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at W. Kerr Scott Reservoir (WLKN7) reported 2.39 inches for the 24-hours ending at 700 AM local time, which was the highest on record for December 21st. Continuous climate records at this station exist since 1965.

Rain and snow developed across the Great Smoky Mountains, the Nantahala Mountains, and surrounding areas during the evening of the 8th and continued into the overnight. While precipitation fell as mostly snow above 3500 feet or so, mostly rain (with snow mixing in at times) fell in the valleys. By daybreak on the 9th, accumulations of 2 to 5 inches were common in the high elevations, while valley accumulations were generally less than an inch.

Rain and snow developed shortly after midnight across the North Carolina Piedmont, becoming all snow in most areas by daybreak, with some sleet mixing in along and south of the I-85 corridor. Snow, moderate to heavy at times, continued across the North Carolina Piedmont through the morning of the 9th, before tapering off (or changing to rain) by early afternoon. Sleet and rain mixed in with the snow at times, especially closer to the I-85 corridor, including much of Charlotte and vicinity. This cut down on storm total amounts in those areas, where amounts were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range. However, totals north and west of the city generally range from 4 to 6 inches, with locally higher amounts of up t0 8 inches reported closer to I-40.

This resulted in flooding of the French Broad River and some of its larger tributaries, as well as on a few other streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stream gauges near the headwaters of the French Broad River as well as multiple tributaries, including Davidson River and Little River, exceeded their established flood stages after 4 to 6 inches of rain fell throughout the basin from the afternoon of the 27th through the 28th. The French Broad River flooded portions of Hannah Ford Rd and Island Ford Rd, as well as Depot St and Highway 276 in Rosman. Davidson River flooded Davidson River Rd and Wilson Rd in Pisgah Forest. Little River also flooded Merrill Lane. While flood waters receded on most streams by late on the 28th, flooding persisted on the Little River for another day, while river flooding continued on the French Broad downstream of Brevard and Pisgah Forest until January 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over eastern North America stalled Florence's forward motion a few miles off the southeast North Carolina coast on September 13th. Despite making landfall as a weakened|Category 1 hurricane, Florence still produced 40 to 70 mph wind gusts, enough wind speed to uproot trees and cause widespread power outages throughout the Carolinas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph resulted in numerous trees down across Scotland County, including on homes, cars, power lines and damage to structures. Numerous customers lost power in Scotland County as a result of the tropical storm force winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another southern stream dominated system emerged from the southwestern U.S. with a complex surface low tracking from the Gulf Coast into the southern Mid-Atlantic December 20-22. This was again an all liquid event, with 2-day rainfall totals (ending 12z, or 7 AM EST, on 12/22) ranging from 1 to 4 inches with isolated higher amounts mainly along and near the Blue Ridge. The top five rainfall reports in northwest North Carolina in the 48-hours ending 700 AM local time on the 22nd included Boone RG (BONN7) 4.62 inches, Glade Creek VFD DCP (GLCN7) 4.54 inches, Sandy Flats IFLOWS (SDRN7) 4.24 inches, Sparta 3.5 SSW CCR (NC-AG-1) 4.19 inches and Blowing Rock 4.4 NE CCR (NC-WT-6) 3.93 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Jefferson 2 E (JFFN7) reported 2.35 inches for the 24-hours ending at 700 AM local time which was the highest on record for December 21st. The old record was 1.73 inches in 1957. It was also the 7th highest daily precipitation recorded in the month of December. The record is 3.18 inches set December 2, 2010. Nearly continuous climate records at this station started in 1910.

Flooding damaged approximately 579 structures throughout the county, destroying 1 and resulting in over $1.5 million in property damage and likely $5 million or more in crop damage. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow developed shortly after midnight across the North Carolina Piedmont, becoming all snow in most areas by daybreak, with some sleet mixing in along and south of the I-85 corridor. Snow, moderate to heavy at times, continued across the North Carolina Piedmont through the morning of the 9th, before tapering off (or changing to rain) by early afternoon. Sleet and rain mixed in with the snow at times, especially closer to the I-85 corridor, including much of Charlotte and vicinity. This cut down on storm total amounts in those areas, where amounts were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range. However, totals north and west of the city generally range from 4 to 6 inches, with locally higher amounts of up t0 8 inches reported closer to I-40.

A low pressure system tracked eastward along the Gulf Coast and spread moisture northward toward the Mid Atlantic.  Meanwhile, high pressure to the north allowed temperatures to fall into the 20s, which caused the moisture to mostly fall as heavy snow.  Some sleet and freezing drizzle also fell during this storm.  The heavy snow caused numerous vehicle accidents and downed trees that fell on to roads and power lines, and it also caused one indirect fatality in Yadkin County.  Average snowfall accumulations ranged from ten to twenty inches over northwest North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 16.0 inches at West Kerr Scott Reservoir to 17.8 inches at North Wilkesboro.  The storm caused 26 vehicle accidents and stranded 41 motorists.  The weight of the snow downed 57 trees, and there were 29 electrical hazards reported mostly due to trees on power lines.  In addition, about 20 people from five families that also included small children were stranded in their homes without power for three days.  Several people from the North Carolina National Guard rescued the families and took them to a shelter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 12.0 inches about six miles east of Yadkinville to 14.5 inches at Boonville.  One indirect fatality occurred when a male truck driver got stuck on Interstate 77 several miles north of the Route 421 intersection due to the heavy snow.  He tried to dig his tractor trailer out of the snow but suffered a heart attack while shoveling.  The man later died at a nearby hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of rain and snow developed during the evening across portions of the southern North Carolina Piedmont, becoming all snow by late evening. The snow continued into the overnight hours, while some sleet mixed in for locations along I-85 after midnight. Snow and sleet continued through the morning of the 9th and by late morning, the precipitation transitioned to rain in some areas south of I-85, but otherwise became light and insignificant in areas farther north. Storm total accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

Tropical cyclone Michael tracked northeast across the Midlands and Piedmont of South Carolina and through the North Carolina Piedmont on the 11th. Three to six inches of rain falling in around 6 hours or less resulted in localized flash flooding, mainly in locations near the Blue Ridge and also across the far western Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported flash flooding developed, mainly along small streams in the Old Fort area after 4 to 6 inches of rain fell in just a few hours. The main creeks impacted were Guthrie Branch which flooded a portion of Branson Creek Dr. Crooked Creek also flooded a portion of Hebron Rd where a swift water rescue became necessary. By late morning, heavy rain falling in and near the headwaters of the Catawba River caused the river to rise above its established flood stage at Pleasant Gardens.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms and stream gauges indicated flash flooding developed along the Cane River and South Toe River basins after 4 to 7 inches of rain fell in the headwaters in just a few hours. More than a dozen roads were flooded and/or closed across central and southern portions of the county, including Highway 80 east of Burnsville and State Rd 1112 south of Burnsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across northwest North Carolina around midnight the morning of the 9th, and began accumulating quickly. Moderate to heavy snow continued through the morning of the 9th before tapering off during the early afternoon. Storm total accumulations were generally in the 10 to 15 inch range, with slightly lower amounts south of I-40, and locally higher amounts across the mountains, particularly the high peaks along the Blue Ridge, where more than two feet fell. Travel was paralyzed across this area for a couple of days.

As the storm moved inland, from September 15 to 17,  heavy rain of 10 to 25 inches  caused widespread inland flooding, inundating cities such as Fayetteville, Smithfield, Goldsboro, Durham, and Chapel Hill, and causing major river flooding on main-stem rivers such as the Neuse, Cape Fear, and Little River. Most major roads and highways in the area experienced some flooding, with large stretches of I-40 and I-95 remaining impassable for days after the storm had passed. The storm also spawned tornadoes in several places along its path. There were 3 direct and 6 indirect deaths attributed to the storm with in the WFO RAH CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall of 10 to 15 inches caused widespread flooding across the county. Additional rainfall upstream caused moderate flooding along the Eno River near Enon. Additionally, water held in Jordan Lake from rainfall in its headwaters resulted in flooding along the lake into portions of the county, which lingered for several days. Flooding damaged approximately 638 structures throughout the county, destroying 4 and resulting in $11.05 million in property damage. Numerous roads were closed due to flooding. Numerous homes and businesses were flooded as well. While final losses on crops are not yet tallied, estimates around $5 million are possible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across northwest North Carolina around midnight the morning of the 9th, and began accumulating quickly. Moderate to heavy snow continued through the morning of the 9th before tapering off during the early afternoon. Storm total accumulations were generally in the 10 to 15 inch range, with slightly lower amounts south of I-40, and locally higher amounts across the mountains, particularly the high peaks along the Blue Ridge, where more than two feet fell. Travel was paralyzed across this area for a couple of days.

A cold and moist northwest flow developing across the Tennessee Valley and the southern Appalachians in the wake of a cold front produced scattered to numerous snow showers across the northern mountains and Madison County throughout the morning and into the afternoon of the 5th. By the time the snow showers tapered off to flurries, total accumulations generally ranged from 1 to 4 inches across the area. However, some locations across the high elevations along the Tennessee border reported as much as 6 inches, while the valleys and lower spots near the Blue Ridge barely saw a dusting.

Rain changed to snow across the high elevations of the North Carolina mountains as freezing levels quickly fell in the wake of a strong cold front. A cold and moist northwest flow developing in the wake of the front across the Tennessee Valley and the southern Appalachians resulted in scattered to numerous snow showers continuing near the Tennessee border throughout the afternoon and evening of the 21st, through the morning of the 22nd. Accumulations were mainly confined to elevations above 3500 feet, where 1 to 3 inches generally fell. Some locations above 5000 feet reported as much as a half foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical cyclone Michael tracked northeast across the Midlands and Piedmont of South Carolina and through the North Carolina Piedmont on the 11th. Three to six inches of rain falling in around 6 hours or less resulted in localized flash flooding, mainly in locations near the Blue Ridge and also across the far western Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple stream gauges indicated flash flooding developed on streams across central and eastern Henderson County after 4 to 6 inches of rain fell in just a few hours. Flooding first developed along the Rocky Broad basin in the Bat Cave area, where a stream gauge exceeded moderate flood stage, indicating flooding of some roads and campgrounds and threatening of structures near the gauge site. Bat Fork Creek also reached moderate flood stage in Hendersonville, indicating flooding of roads and some runways in the vicinity of the Henderson County airport, including New Hope Rd, Airport Rd, and Tracey Grove Rd. Mud Creek also briefly flooded in Hendersonville, flooding parking lots near the intersection of South King St and South Church St.
